029-lomax-field-recorder: In the 1930s and 1940s, a young collector drove long roads with recording equipment heavy enough to change the whole trip.

049-strange-side-business: In a British seaside town in the 1970s, a woman entrepreneur opened a small shop where customers brought empty bottles back to be refilled.

052-stream-after-rain: In mid-twentieth-century America, a marine biologist received more and more letters: there were fewer birds, dead fish, and land that felt wrong after spraying.

071-flickering-hall-light: In twentieth-century Sydney, the shell roof of an opera house drew the architect, government, and engineering team into the same difficulty.
072-soup-that-travels: In a Detroit factory in 1913, the automobile chassis no longer waited silently for workers to gather around it, but moved slowly along a line.

081-balcony-tomato-plan: In an eighteenth-century English field, a farmer saw seed being scattered by hand, with much eaten by birds and much falling at the wrong depth.
082-fallen-branch-trail: In late nineteenth-century America, a young man returned from studying forestry in Europe and saw vast forests being governed by the logic of short-term cutting.
083-fish-market-morning: In the late twentieth century, a fish researcher heard young fishers say the catch was still normal, yet found a very different ocean in old records.

088-farm-visit-class: In late twentieth-century ranches and slaughterhouses, a young woman stood beside livestock passages and noticed that cattle were frightened by reflections, shadows, and sudden turns.
089-unusual-animal-garden: In the early twenty-first century on Norway's Svalbard archipelago, people carried seed samples from around the world into a vault inside a frozen mountain.

092-key-under-the-mat: In Britain in 1942, while the war was still going on, an economist placed poverty, disease, unemployment, and old age into the same report.

102-clean-hands-station: In nineteenth-century German cities, dense population, sewage, and infectious disease made it impossible for medicine to remain only beside the sickbed.
103-night-watch-call: On nineteenth-century London streets, rapid urban expansion, crime, poverty, crowding, and public fear made older forms of policing increasingly inadequate.

108-festival-operations-plan: In the 1980s, a researcher observed service sites and found that customers saw only the part of the whole system that rose above the surface.
109-service-that-had-no-name: In 1950s London, a pastor read in the newspaper about young people driven toward despair by isolation and shame, and began imagining a telephone line anyone could call.

0113-teacher-training-without-subject-sp: In July 1839, when a new school opened in Lexington, Massachusetts, the first group of students was so small that it hardly looked like a school.

0212-fashion-interior-and-industrial-des: In nineteenth-century Britain, a young man who loved poetry and medieval stories entered rooms of the industrial age and kept feeling that everyday objects were losing the human hand.
0213-fine-arts: In late nineteenth-century southern France, the son of a banking family returned again and again to the canvas, staring at apples, tablecloths, and a mountain.
0214-handicrafts: In early twentieth-century Japan, as cities modernized rapidly, a young thinker paused before Korean ceramics and ordinary folk objects.
0215-music-and-performing-arts: In late nineteenth-century Moscow, a young man who loved the stage stood in the theatre and grew increasingly unable to bear exaggerated, hollow performances concerned only with showing off the voice.
0219-arts-not-elsewhere-classified: In New York in 1917, an artist turned an ordinary plumbing fixture around, signed it with a pseudonym, and sent it to an exhibition that claimed to be open.
0221-religion-and-theology: In Germany in 1517, a young theology teacher heard ordinary people talk about indulgences and became increasingly uneasy.
0222-history-and-archaeology: In the dust of Egypt's Valley of the Kings in 1922, a man who had long worked on excavations had nearly lost his funding, yet still asked workers to keep clearing what looked like ordinary ground.
0223-philosophy-and-ethics: In fifth-century BCE Athens, a man from an ordinary craft family often spoke with people in marketplaces, gymnasia, and banquets.
0229-humanities-except-languages-not-els: In the 1920s, a young woman who had left a small town in the American South returned with anthropological training to familiar porches and street corners.

0232-literature-and-linguistics: In early twentieth-century Geneva, a language teacher repeatedly reminded students in class that language is not a list of names for objects.

0313-psychology: In Leipzig in 1879, a room held timers, metronomes, reaction keys, and students asked to report their experiences carefully.

0412-finance-banking-and-insurance: In the late seventeenth century, a man used to calculating comet orbits began reading one city's records of births and deaths seriously.

0512-biochemistry: In late nineteenth-century Berlin, a researcher crushed and filtered yeast cells, originally only wanting to preserve an extract, but found that the liquid began bubbling on its own.
0519-biological-and-related-sciences-not: In a mid-twentieth-century cornfield, a woman scientist observed kernel colors one by one and recorded patterns that changed like spots.
0521-environmental-sciences: On Mauna Loa in Hawaii in 1958, a young chemist sent air samples into instruments and cared about stability after the decimal point.
0522-natural-environments-and-wildlife: In a Tanzanian forest in 1960, a young woman sat under trees and waited for a long time until chimpanzees were willing to come close.

01-the-bridge-too-short: In a mountain village, children crossed a stream on stones to reach school.

05-the-lighthouse-and-the-fog: The harbor keeper had an old notebook that recorded storms.

08-the-orchard-that-kept-notebooks: An orchard grew at the edge of a dry plain.

well-runs-low: The well did not disappear on a single morning.
bread-price-debate: In the school cafeteria, a new lunch set cost only three euros. Students stood in a long line, and the menu board said, "Affordable for everyone."
school-curriculum-debate: Lina brought three lesson plans and expected the meeting to end with a quick vote.
broad-street-pump: In 1854 London Soho, the streets were narrow, the houses crowded, and the air held the smell of sewage and dampness.

cuban-missile-crisis-excomm: In October 1962, U-2 reconnaissance photographs lay spread across a conference table, showing launch sites still under construction in black and white.
